ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for 'cereal with milk' as a combined dish; 'cereal food product' (FOODON_03315873) is the closest defensible parent class. The milk component is not captured in this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A000E (Cereals and cereal products) is the closest defensible category for this mixed cereal-and-milk dish. A combined cereal-with-milk composite code does not exist in FoodEx2; the milk component is not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for cereal with milk as a combined food item was identified in the standard clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 250 g, representing roughly 30 g of sweetened breakfast cereal combined with 220 ml of plain whole milk in a standard breakfast bowl.

nutrient_source.title: Cereal with milk — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Cereal with milk' (record F05050013). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record and has been estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Cereals, ready-to-eat, with whole milk' (NDB 08120) as an analogous composite; the estimate is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 31 is sourced from the input record citing the Embedded GI Database, cross-referenced against the University of Sydney GI Database for ready-to-eat sweetened cereal with whole milk. The University of Sydney GI Database lists mixed cereal-with-milk preparations in the low GI range (GI 30–40 depending on cereal type). A GI of 31 is defensible for a sweetened cereal with milk composite. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (31 × 19.71 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 15.3 for a 250 g serving (carbohydrate contribution ~49.3 g per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.65 g/100 g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g) and very low potassium (1 mg/100 g), creating an unfavourable sugar-to-potassium ratio that may promote sodium retention and elevate blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sweetened cereal with milk due to its high sugar and near-absent potassium; replacing with plain oats or unsweetened muesli with low-fat milk provides a substantially better sodium-potassium balance and fibre profile for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 9.65 g/100 g combined with very low dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g) results in rapid postprandial glucose absorption; despite a low GI of 31, the high sugar load and minimal fibre make portion control critical for glycaemic management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit sweetened cereal with milk; if consumed, restrict to a 30 g cereal portion with low-fat milk and pair with a high-fibre food to blunt the glycaemic response, or substitute with plain oats or bran cereal which provide substantially more fibre and lower sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar at 9.65 g/100 g with saturated fat estimated at approximately 2.1 g/100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g) contributes to an atherogenic dietary pattern when consumed regularly.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit sweetened cereal with milk due to its high sugar and low fibre content; choosing unsweetened whole-grain cereal or oats with low-fat milk reduces added sugar and saturated fat intake while increasing dietary fibre, supporting better cardiovascular outcomes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (9.65 g/100 g) with near-zero dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g) and absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid sweetened cereal with milk and instead choose plain oats with walnuts, chia seeds, and berries, which provide anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, and dietary fibre.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cereal with milk is a common breakfast dish consisting of ready-to-eat sweetened breakfast cereal served in a bowl with plain whole milk. The cereal component is typically a processed grain product (wheat, corn, or rice-based) that has been fortified and sweetened during manufacture; the milk component is plain liquid cow's milk. The dish requires no cooking — the cereal is simply poured into a bowl and milk is added. The texture is a combination of crunchy cereal pieces that gradually soften in the milk, with a sweet, mildly creamy flavour. Per 100 g, the dish provides 129 kcal, 3.72 g protein, 3.78 g fat, 19.71 g carbohydrate, 9.65 g sugar, 0.3 g dietary fibre, 101 mg sodium, 100 mg calcium, and 1 mg iron. The high sugar content (9.65 g/100 g) combined with very low dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g) means rapid glucose absorption, making this dish a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The calcium content (100 mg/100 g) from the milk component is a positive nutritional feature. The glycemic index is estimated at 31 (low), but the glycemic load per 250 g serving is approximately 15.3, which is moderate. Clinically, this dish is not recommended for diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis management due to its high sugar and negligible fibre profile. It is widely consumed as a quick breakfast in Singapore and internationally, particularly by children and working adults.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat cereal with milk?
How many calories in a bowl of cereal with milk?
Is cereal with milk good for hypertension?
What is the GI of cereal with milk?
Is cereal with milk suitable for weight loss?
How much sugar is in cereal with milk?
Can I eat cereal with milk if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (canonical primary source for Singapore food records, record F05050013) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as an analogous composite and is clearly documented. GI is cross-referenced against the University of Sydney GI Database for cereal-with-milk composites and is defensible at GI 31.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.1 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for ready-to-eat sweetened cereal with whole milk (NDB 08120) as the HPB record does not report saturated fat separately. This is an analogous estimate and should be updated when HPB publishes a disaggregated fatty acid profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, water content, and micronutrient panel beyond calcium and iron are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this composite dish as of 2026. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 08120) was consulted but the composite cereal-with-milk entry does not disaggregate all micronutrients in a form directly transferable to this record without introducing unacceptable estimation uncertainty. 3. Potassium is reported as 1 mg/100 g in the HPB source, which is unusually low for a cereal-with-milk composite (whole milk alone contributes approximately 150 mg/100 g potassium). This value is preserved as reported from the canonical HPB source but flagged for review; it may reflect a data entry anomaly in the original HPB record. 4. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g in the HPB source. Whole milk typically contributes approximately 10–14 mg cholesterol per 100 g of composite; this value is preserved as reported from the canonical HPB source but is flagged as potentially underreported. Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per the HPB source record. 5. The input dietary_tags included 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie'; sodium at 101 mg/100 g is borderline for a low-sodium classification and these tags have been omitted from the output classification pending review. 6. GI source confidence in the input was 0.375, reflecting uncertainty in the original embedded GI database; the GI of 31 has been cross-referenced against the University of Sydney GI Database for cereal-with-milk composites and is considered defensible as an analogous estimate.
